Then the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa rose from his seat and arranged his upper robe on one shoulder, knelt with his right knee on the ground, and said to the venerable Mahākātyāyana, “I have seen you, preceptor, and I have experienced the Blessed One’s Dharma body as well. But since I have not seen his physical body, preceptor, I would go to see the Blessed One’s physical body.”

“Do so, my son. It is rare to see the one thus gone, the arhat, the complete and perfect Buddha‍—as rare as a flower on an udumbara tree. Bow your head at the feet of the Blessed One on my behalf ask him if he is at ease, and so forth,A standard formula, abbreviated here, but see 1.169. and then pose these five questions:In the following, Mahākātyāyana instructs Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa to ask the Buddha “five questions” (Tib. zhu ba lnga bo; Skt. pañca praśnāni) but the third of these questions is omitted in both the Sanskrit and Tibetan texts. While the third question is never explicitly stated in the text, the Buddha addresses five issues in his response to Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa, the third of which concerns sandals with lining. There on 1.180, the Buddha allows that sandals with a single lining but not a second or third lining may be kept and worn (Tib. mchil lham rim gcig pa bcang bar bya ste / rim gnyis pa ma yin / rim gsum pa ma yin no; Skt. ekapalāśike upānahe dhārayitavye na dvipuṭe na tripuṭe). The section in which the Buddha allows sandals with a single lining can be found on 1.275.
“Honorable One, as the company of monks in the frontier country of Aśmaka is only small, it is difficult to fill a gathering of ten for an assembly. What should I do about this?

“Honorable One, the ground grows rough after it is trampled by cattle. In a place like the frontier country of Aśmaka, ground-spreads and ground-spread covers are made from sheepskin, cowhide, deerskin, and goatskin. Just as in other lands the ground-spreads and ground-spread covers are seats of wool, seats of tree bark, seats of cotton, and seats of cotton-wool mix, just so in the frontier country of Aśmaka, the ground-spreads and ground-spread covers are sheepskin, and so forth, as just mentioned.
“The people, who wash with water, have a custom of taking ritual baths.

“Who incurs an offense requiring forfeiture, if the robes sent by a monk to another monk are lost on their way from the starting point and do not reach the destination?”This refers presumably to the first of the thirty offenses requiring forfeiture (Skt. naiḥsargikāḥ pātayantikāḥ; Tib. spang ba’i ltung byed). The first of these offenses, abbreviated as Tib. gos ’chang ba; Skt. cīvara, deals with keeping cloth that is not one’s own for ten days or more. The relevant offense is given in The Prātimokṣa Sūtra, (Toh 2, F.8.a): Tib. dge slong chos gos zin pas sra brkyang phyung na / zhag bcu’i bar du gos lhag pa rnams par ma brtags pa bcang bar bya’o // de las ’das par bcangs na spang ba’i ltung byed do. The Sanskrit original reads Skt. niṣṭhitacīvareṇa bhikṣuṇā uddhṛte kaṭhine daśāha­paramaṃ atirekacīvaram avi­kalpitaṃ dhārayitavyaṃ tataḥ uttari dhārayen naisargikā pāyantikā.

The venerable Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa listened without speaking to the venerable Mahākātyāyana. Then the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa bowed his head at the feet of the venerable Mahākātyāyana and took his leave from the venerable Mahākātyāyana.

When that very night had passed and morning come, the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa put on his lower garment and, taking his bowl and robe, went into the village of Vāsava for alms and then gradually made his way to Śrāvastī. Then, having put away his bowl and robe and washed his feet, the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa went to the Blessed One, and on arrival bowed his head at the feet of the Blessed One and sat to one side.
Then the Blessed One said to the venerable Ānanda, “Go, Ānanda! Prepare a couch in one of the monastery buildings for the One Thus Gone and Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa.”

The venerable Ānanda replied, “Honorable One, I shall do as you say.” Following the Blessed One’s order, he prepared a couch in one of the monastery buildings for the One Thus Gone and Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa. He went to the Blessed One, and on arrival said to the Blessed One, “Honorable One, I have prepared a couch for the One Thus Gone and Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa in one of the monastery buildings. Blessed One, it is time.”

The Blessed One and Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa then went to the monastery building. Having washed his feet outside the monastery building, the Blessed One entered, sat down cross-legged with his back straight, and focused his recollection. The venerable Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa also washed his feet outside the building, and, on entering, with his legs crossed and body straight, focused his recollection. The Blessed One and the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa passed the night in noble silence.
Then, as dawn approached, the Blessed One said to the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa, “Śroṇa, be confident in the Dharma I have declared. After perceiving it, I awoke to buddhahood.”

At the invitation of the Blessed One, Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa then recited, in the deep timbre of the frontier country of Aśmaka, from The Inspired Utterances,Tib. ched du brjod pa; Skt. Udāna. For this work and the others chanted by Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa, see Lamotte 1988, 161–63. Passing to the Farther Shore,Tib. pha rol ’gro byed; Skt. Pārāyaṇa. Seeing the Truth,Tib. bden pa mthong ba; Skt. Satyadṛś. The Verses of Śaila,Tib. ri gnas kyi tshigs su bcad pa; Skt. Śailagāthā. The Verses of the Sage,Tib. thub pa’i tshigs su bcad pa; Skt. Munigāthā. The Verses of the Elders,Tib. gnas brtan gyi tshigs su bcad pa; Skt. Sthaviragāthā. The Verses of the Elder Nuns,Tib. gnas brtan ma’i tshigs su bcad pa; Skt. Sthavirīgāthā. and Sūtras Concerning the Goal.Tib. don gyi tshoms kyi mdo sde dag; Skt. Artha­vargīyāṇi sūtrāṇi.

When the Blessed One knew that Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa had finished speaking, he said to the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa, “Good, Śroṇa, good. You have recited sweetly the Dharma that I have declared. After perceiving it, I awoke to buddhahood.”
Then the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa thought to himself, “Now the time has come for me to deliver to the Blessed One the requests sent by my preceptor.”

With this in mind, he rose from his seat, placed his upper robe over one shoulder, and with his palms pressed together, bowed in the direction of the Blessed One and said to the Blessed One, “Honorable One, in a village in the frontier country of Aśmaka called Vāsava lives the honorable Mahākātyāyana, my preceptor, and he bows with his head at the feet of the Blessed One. He asks after your health, whether you are free from trouble, free from disease, in good physical condition, healthy, strong, happy, and so at ease.”This passage is a standard formula. It is abbreviated in the Skt. MS, but the full Sanskrit form can be supplied from a similar passage in the Divyāvadāna 156.13–15.
The Blessed One said, “Śroṇa, may the monk Kātyāyana be happy! You should also relate in detail the five questions.”

Then the Blessed One said to Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa, “Śroṇa, since now is not the time for me to expound on this question, ask your questions in the presence of the saṅgha. That will be the time for expounding on what you ask.”
The Blessed One rose early in the morning and sat down on the prepared seat in the midst of the saṅgha. Then the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa, and so forth, up to sat to one side.

Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa said to the Blessed One, “Honorable One, in a village in the frontier country of Aśmaka called Vāsava lives the honorable Mahākātyāyana, my preceptor, and he bows at the feet of the Blessed One, and so forth, up to at ease.”
He related in detail the five questions as above,See F.264.b. up to “offense requiring forfeiture.”

The Blessed One said, “I therefore allow that ordination in border regions may be done by a group of five monks that includes a vinaya holder. Monks may bathe regularly. Sandals with a single lining may be kept, but not with a second lining, and not a third. If they wear out, they should be patched and then worn. Leather may be kept. If the robes sent by a monk to another monk are lost on their way from the starting point and do not reach the destination, no one will be guilty of an offense requiring forfeiture.”

The venerable Upāli said to the Blessed One, “Honorable One, the Blessed One has said ordination in the border regions may be done by a group of five monks that includes a vinaya holder. What is the heartland, and what are border regions?”

“Upāli, in the east, on the eastern side of the town called Puṇḍravardhana,Also called Puṇḍavardhana, located in present-day West Bengal; see BHSD, p. 346; Rotman 2008, p. 400, n. 192. there is a forest called Puṇḍrakakṣa. That is the border.Tib. dbus; Skt. has anta. Beyond that are the border regions. In the south, on the far side of the town called Śarāvatī,Also called Sarāvatī; see BHSD, p. 583. there is a river called Śarāvatī; that is the border. Beyond that are the border regions. In the west, the brahmin towns of SthūṇaBHSD, p. 611. and UpasthūṇaBHSD, p. 144. are the border. Beyond that are the border regions. In the north, the mountain called UśīragiriPali Usiraddhaja. is the border. Beyond that are the border regions.”

div.head: Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa’s Past Life
div.p: The monks had doubts and asked the Blessed Buddha, who cuts all doubts, “Honorable One, what deed did the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa do that ripened into his being born in a rich, wealthy, prosperous family, with a jeweled earring attached to his ear, and, having seen the miserable realms, into his going forth in the Blessed One’s teachings, after which he abandoned all the afflictions and realized arhatship?”

“Monks,” replied the Blessed One, “Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa himself performed the actions, and so forth, as above, up to…See The Chapter on Going Forth 4.306–4.307 for the full text of this formula. are right, they alight upon embodied beings. Monks, in a time now passed, during this fortunate eon, when the lifespan of beings was twenty thousand years, the blessed Buddha Kāśyapa appeared in the world, a teacher, a one thus gone, an arhat, a perfectly awakened buddha, and so forth, up to…who knew the world.See The Chapter on Going Forth 4.306–4.307 for the full text of this formula.

“In the town of Vārāṇasī lived a married couple, both of whom went for refuge and received the training precepts from the perfectly awakened Buddha Kāśyapa. When the perfectly awakened Buddha Kāśyapa had completed all the work of a buddha and passed beyond suffering into the expanse of nirvāṇa without remainder of aggregates, King Kṛkin had a stūpa built for him, made of four sorts of jewels. It was a league in circumference and half a league in height. The king gave the taxes collected at the gate on the eastern side of the city to that stūpa in order to make good any disrepair.

“After King Kṛkin’s death, his son called Sujāta was put on the throne. His ministers rendered a tiny amount in tax revenue to him. He said to the ministers, ‘Why do you render a tiny amount in tax revenue to me? Are taxes not collected in my country?’

“They replied, ‘Your Majesty, where can taxes be collected from? Your Majesty, those taxes that are collected from the gate on the eastern side of the city are to be used for making good any disrepair to your royal father’s stūpa. If Your Majesty approves, we will do away with that tax.’

“He said, ‘Gentlemen, what was done by my father, that was done by Brahmā; that was done by Indra.’This statement seems to imply the divine right of kings, i.e. that a king rules with the imprimatur of the gods and thus a king’s wishes are inviolable. See Rotman 2008, p. 400, n. 201, for the difference between the text in the Carmavastu and the Divyāvadāna; the statement is in the negative in the latter.

“They thought, ‘If His Majesty approves, we will ensure that those particular taxes are not collected.’ With this in mind, they arranged for the gate to be shut off and for the collection of taxes to stop from then on.
“That stūpa fell into disrepair again, while the couple who had grown old continued to do the sweeping for the stūpa.
“A merchant from the north took up his goods and, arriving in Vārāṇasī, saw that stūpa that had fallen into disrepair. On seeing it, he asked the couple, ‘Father, mother, this stūpa, whose is it?’
“They replied, ‘It is the perfectly awakened Buddha Kāśyapa’s.’
“ ‘Who made it?’
“ ‘King Kṛkin.’
“ ‘That King Kṛkin, did he not make a small donation for making good any disrepair to the stūpa?’

“The couple replied, ‘There was a donation, in that he gave the taxes collected from the eastern gate of the city to make good the stūpa when it fell into disrepair. After his death, his son named Sujāta was put on the throne. Because he did away with the collection of that tax, the stūpa fell into disrepair.’

“The merchant took a jeweled earring from his ear and gave it to the couple, saying, ‘Father, mother, make good the disrepair to this stūpa with this precious earring. When I’ve traded my goods, I will come back and give you more.’
“The couple sold the earring and, after repairing the dilapidated stūpa, they had something left over.

“Some time later, after the merchant had traded his goods, he came back, bringing the profit. He saw that that stūpa wanted for nothing. Just looking at it, he rejoiced even more and gained faith, asking, ‘Father, mother, did you not incur some debt?’
“The couple replied, ‘Son, we haven’t incurred any debt at all; in fact, there is something left over.’

“He gained great faith and gave what was left over and a further sum to the stūpa. Paying great homage to the stūpa with offerings, he made the aspiration, ‘By this virtuous root, may I be reborn into a rich, wealthy, prosperous family. May I attain a Dharma such as this. May I please just such a teacher, while not displeasing him.’

“Monks, what do you think? That merchant was this very Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa. He who served the stūpa of the perfectly awakened Buddha Kāśyapa was born into a rich, wealthy, prosperous family, with a jeweled earring attached to his ear, through the ripening of that act of making an aspiration. He went forth in accordance with my teachings and, after abandoning all afflictions, he achieved arhatship. He has pleased me, a teacher who is equal in strength to the perfectly awakened Buddha Kāśyapa, equal in power, equal in diligence, possessing the quality that is equal to the unequaled, and he has not displeased me.

“The ripened fruits of wholly negative actions are wholly negative, while the ripened fruits of wholly positive actions are wholly positive, and the ripened fruits of mixed actions are mixed. Monks, therefore abandon wholly negative and mixed actions and seek wholly positive actions. Monks, this is how you should train.”
“Honorable One, what deed did the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa do that led to his seeing the miserable realms in this very lifetime?”
The Blessed One said, “He spoke harshly to his mother. It is due to the ripening of that verbal act that he saw the miserable realms in this very lifetime.”

div.head: Wide Beds
div.p: The Blessed Buddha was staying in Śrāvastī, in Prince Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada’s Park. The venerable Upananda, who was new in the saṅgha, received, in accordance with his rank,Before every monsoon, a monastery’s monk residence caretaker assigns dwellings (Tib. gnas khang) and bedding (Tib. mal cha) to the monks in order of seniority (as determined by date of ordination), starting with the preceptors and instructors; see The Chapter on the Rains Retreat (Toh 1-4), 1.17–1.26. Upananda is here described as “new,” that is, a new monk. In The Chapter on Going Forth, at the end of the ordination rite, monks are “enjoined to dwell in tranquility” with their monastic brothers. They are encouraged to respect the saṅgha’s hierarchy of seniority, which includes elders (Tib. gnas brtan; Skt. sthavira), those who are middling (Tib. bar ma; Skt. madhyama), and newcomers (Tib. gsar bu). Dwellings and their furnishings are the subject of The Chapters on Monastic Discipline’s fifteenth chapter, The Chapter on Dwellings and Seating. For a study and translation of the first half of this chapter into English, see Schopen 2000. a bed frame,Mattresses are to be placed on a “bed frame” (Tib. khri; Skt. khaṭṭā or maṃcaka). Here, in The Chapter on Leather, “mattress” represents the Sanskrit terms śayyā and śayanāsana, which are rendered into Tibetan with mal cha, mal stan, khri stan, gnas mal che, gdan, and stan. In each case, the term refers to a cushioned seat or bed, i.e., a mattress. In the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, the Sanskrit term śayanāsana has several meanings, including (1) “mattress, bedding,” i.e., the soft parts of a bed; (2) “seating, furniture,” i.e., a couch installed in a monastery and used for either sleeping or sitting on in the hall; and (3) “dwelling, residence.” It has commonly been translated “bedding and seating,” though “dwelling and bedding” may be more apt. which happened to be wobbly. Out of caution, he tried not to roll over as he slept. He thought, “I, Upananda, am attached even to couches. If I do not exchange the mattress or the bed frame, my name is not Upananda!”
With this in mind, he got up early in the morning and presented himself to King Prasenajit of Kośala, who said to him, “Welcome, Honorable Upananda. Has the honorable Upananda slept well?”

Upananda replied, “You pig!Tib. gsus po che (Skt. gillapeṭha and gillapeṭṭa) is an epithet meaning “pot-bellied.” It is related to the Skt. Lambodara, an epithet of Gaṇeśa, which also carries the implication of “glutton.” Edgerton (BHSD, p. 212) says that here gillapeṭṭa is used as a term of abuse. We have elected to translate it with “You pig!” This expression is certainly remarkable, and it is possible that Upananda was cloaking his insolence in faux respect with an expression akin to Your Rotundness. At 1.202, gsus po che appears again in the Tibetan, this time appended with ko sa la’i yul; see Tib. gsus po che ko sa la’i yul rnams kho bo dbang ngam ci. The corresponding Sanskrit, however, does not include gillapeṭha; see kiṃ mama kosalo janapado vartate. What do you think? How am I, Upananda, who is used to sleeping on an eight-layered mattress? As I am new in the saṅgha, I received, in accordance with my rank, a bed frame, which happens to be wobbly. Out of caution, I try not to roll over as I sleep.”

“Honorable Upananda, an eight-layer mattress like this is suitable, it is highly suitable, it is superbly suitable. Where will it be prohibited? Who could prohibit it? If it is suitable, I give it to you. Take it and go on your way!”
Upananda said, “You pig! Are the lands of Kośala my own? Have it delivered to the monastery and give it to me there!”

Prasenajit gave him eight men. Four carried the mattress and four carried the bed frame. Brahmins and householders saw them walking in the middle of the road and upon seeing them, asked, “Gentlemen, where are you taking His Majesty’s bed today?”
Upananda replied, “To its home.”
“Why are you taking this bed?”
“These were obtained by me,” said Upananda.
The brahmins and householders chastised, rebuked, and criticized him, saying, “Gentlemen, the ascetic Upananda is obsessed with sense pleasures, is he not?”

So Upananda returned to his monastery dwelling, sprinkled water, swept, and spread a coat of fresh cow dung on the floor of the monastery dwelling. Then he arranged his mattress and was preparing to lie down when the Blessed One arrived.
Upananda grew excited and said to him, “Honorable One, please look at the bed I have gotten!”Reading Stok Palace bdag gi… in preference to Degé bdag gis gnas mal ci ’dra ba mchis gzigs su gsol.

Then the Blessed One said to the monks, “Monks, first of all, Upananda should be seen as a deluded person who conceals what is rotten.”“Deluded person who conceals what is rotten” (Tib. rul pa sbed pa ni skyes bu gti mug; Skt. mohapuruṣaḥ prati­gopako). Edgerton (BHSD, p. 362, col. 1) glosses pratigopaka as “concealer, one who acts secretly, on the sly.” There is nothing corresponding to rul pa (“rotten”) in the Skt.

The Blessed One then considered, “The problem that has arisen here is that the monks sleep on high beds and grand beds. That being the case, a monk should not sleep on a high bed or a grand bed. If he does so, he will be guilty of a breach.”

After the Blessed One said that a monk should not sleep on a high bed or a grand bed, a householder invited the saṅgha of monks, the Buddha, and so on to his house and set about arranging high seats.“Seat” (Tib. khri stan; Skt. āsana).

The venerable Ānanda got there in advance and, when he saw him arranging the high seats, he said to him, “Householder, these seats are not appropriate for the monks, so gather them up.” The householder was starting to gather them up when the Blessed One arrived.
Since the Blessed Buddha asks questions even though he knows the answers, the Blessed Buddha inquired of Ānanda, “Ānanda, what are you doing?”

“Blessed One, after this householder had set out seats suitable for great men, I instructed him, ‘Householder, these seats are not appropriate for the monks, so gather them up!’ and now he’s gathering them up.”

“Ānanda, know that such rules will not flourish everywhere, so, Ānanda, do not deter the householder from preparing the seats! That being the case, I allow that a monk may sit on a high seat in a house, but he may not lie on it. In a monastery, he may neither sit nor lie on high seat. If he sits or lies down on one, he will be guilty of a breach.”

div.head: Calf
div.p: A monk who observed the three-robe practiceTib. dge slong chos gos gsum pa; Skt. traicīvarika. The original Vinaya rule established that all monks should own just three robes. Over time, monks were allowed to keep more, while limiting oneself to only three robes became one of a set of thirteen “practices of austerity” or dhūtaguṇa. See Roach 2020, pp. 97–98. came to Śrāvastī from the Southern RouteIn the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, the “Southern Route” (Tib. lho phyogs; Skt. dakṣiṇāpatha) refers to the Deccan region, centered on the capital city at Suvarṇagiri in modern-day Karnataka. See “Chapter IV: Dakkhiṇāpatha or The Deccan and the Far South” of Law’s Geography of Early Buddhism (originally published in 1932, with an e-book edition prepared by Ānandajoti Bhikkhu in 2014). Later in this chapter, under summary 3, some of the Śākyans are said to have fled Virūḍhaka’s assault on Kapilavastu along the “Northern Route” (Tib. nub phyogs; Skt. uttarāpatha). These two routes, the southern and northern, are old trade routes or trunk roads that led out of and into the Buddhist heartland. in order to prostrate at the feet of the Blessed One. When Upananda saw the monk’s fine hide mat he said to him, “Alms seeker,“Alms seeker” (Tib. bsod snyoms pa; Skt. piṇḍapātika). Upananda addresses the monk as a practitioner of another of the thirteen “practices of austerity,” namely, of relying only on alms (and not accepting invitations to meals offered by patrons). See Roach 2020, pp. 111–14. give me that hide! I am going to pay homage at the feet of the Blessed One.”
The monk replied, “Honorable Upananda, regardless of whether you go or not, I have come here to pay homage at the feet of the Blessed One.”
“Alms seeker, what are you thinking? Do you think, ‘Upananda cannot get a hide like this’? Look at all I have gotten!”
“Honorable Upananda, it is precisely because you can get one that I will not give you mine!”

Upananda was acquainted with King Prasenajit of Kośala’s keeper of cattle, Vaḍra by name, so he went to see him, just at the time in the spring when beautiful, multicolored calves were born. Upananda rubbed the back of one of the calves, and Vaḍra said, “Honorable Upananda, do you want to drink the milk of this calf’s mother?”
He replied, “I would like that too, but a monk who has a hide like this could devote himself to virtuous activities comfortably, with few concerns.”

Vaḍra said, “Honorable Upananda, I understand what you have said.The Tibetan translation adds “what you have said” (Tib. bka’ stsal pa) where the Sanskrit indicates only that it has “been understood” (Skt. vijñātam). Now go.”
Upananda left, and Vaḍra instructed his cowherd, “Kill this calf, take the hide, and offer it to the honorable Upananda.”

The cowherd killed the calf, took the hide, and set off. The mother cow followed him, lowing out of distress for her calf. When he entered Prince Jeta’s Grove, the cow stood at the gate of Prince Jeta’s Grove, lowing.

Because blessed buddhas ask questions even though they know the answers, the Blessed Buddha inquired of the venerable Ānanda, “Ānanda, why is this cow standing at the gate of Prince Jeta’s Grove, lowing?”
The venerable Ānanda replied, “Blessed One, the honorable Upananda had the calf killed for its hide. That is why that cow is standing at the gate of Prince Jeta’s Grove, lowing.”
The Blessed One said, “The problem that has arisen here is that the monks keep hides. That being the case, a monk may not keep a hide. If he does so, he will be guilty of a breach.”

After the Blessed One said that a monk may not keep a hide, another householder invited the saṅgha of monks, the Buddha, and so on to a midday meal. As he did not have any other seating to set out, he began setting out seats made from hide.With the phrase “as before” (Skt. pūrvavat), the Sanskrit (pūrvavat tu na sarvatra saṃpatsyate) directs the reader to fill in the relevant material from the parallel passage in the preceding section. The Tibetan translation recites the passage in full, as represented in English translation.

The venerable Ānanda got up early in the morning and went to the householder’s house. When he saw the householder setting out hide seats, he said, “Householder, since these seats are not appropriate for a monk, gather them up!”

The householder was starting to gather the seats up when the Blessed One arrived there. Since blessed buddhas ask questions even though they know the answers, the Blessed Buddha inquired of the venerable Ānanda, “Ānanda, what are you doing?”

The venerable Ānanda replied, “As this householder does not have any other seating to set out, he set out these hide seats. I told him, ‘Householder, these seats are not appropriate for a monk, so gather them up!’ and now he’s gathering up these seats.”

“Ānanda, know that such rules will not prosper everywhere, so, Ānanda, do not deter the householder from setting out the seats. In light of this, I allow that a monk may sit on a leather seat that has been set out in a house, but he may not lie down on it. In a monastery he may neither sit nor lie down on a leather seat. If he sits or lies down on one, he will be guilty of a breach.”

div.head: Carriage, Swimming, Cow’s Tail and Inappropriate Motifs, and Wooden Sandals
Carriage
div.p: The Blessed Buddha was staying in the Bamboo Grove, the Kalandaka Sanctuary, near Rājagṛha.The following story is repeated, with some minor variation in details, in The Chapter on Minor Matters of Monastic Discipline, vol. 10, F.106.a–107.a. From the moment the venerable Pilindavatsa went forth, he was always ill.Venerable Pilindavatsa’s health ordeals are narrated in The Chapter on Minor Matters of Monastic Discipline; see, e.g., F.104.b–106.b. His friends came to see him, and when they got there he expounded the Dharma to them. After listening to the Dharma from the venerable Pilindavatsa, they went to their wives and said, “The noble Pilindavatsa has explained a Dharma like pressed honey, which is a delight to hear.”The Sanskrit for this section stops at the mention of the ever-ill Pilindavatsa and so does not contain the basis for the Tib. sbrang rtsi btsir ba lta bu chos snyan dgu or “pressed honey-like Dharma that is a pleasure to hear.” While Yijing’s Chinese translation has him speak only a “Dharma that is sweet like pure honey” (Ch. shuofa tianmei youru chun mi 説法甜美猶如淳蜜). We have translated the Tibetan dgu as “delight.” It may also be taken as an indicator of plenty, as in the Tib. yod dgu, “all that there is.” Finally, it may indicate how many (“nine”) Dharma topics were discussed, though this last seems unlikely here.

Their wives said, “Your hearing the Dharma from the venerable one is the fruit of a buddha’s appearance. You would like to hear it again but we are embarrassed to go. If the noble one came to us here and expounded the Dharma, we too could listen.”
The men said, “We will go to the noble Pilindavatsa and make the request.” They went to the venerable Pilindavatsa and, on arriving, said, “Noble Pilindavatsa, the women wish to hear the Dharma.”
He replied, “Of course! Am I preventing them?”
They said, “Noble one, the women are embarrassed to come to you. If the noble Pilindavatsa would go there and expound the Dharma, they would be able to listen.”
He replied, “Gentlemen, I cannot go on foot because I am ill.”
They said, “Noble one, travel by carriage!”“Carriage” (Tib. bzhon pa; Skt. yāna).
“My friends, the Blessed One has not allowed that.”
The monks reported this matter to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said, “That being the case, I allow that [a monk] may travel by carriage.”

After the Blessed One said that a monk may travel by carriage, the venerable Pilindavatsa was riding in a carriage, surrounded by his wards and apprentices, when the group of six monks saw him and asked, “Gentlemen, who is that traveler over there?”
The wards and apprentices replied, “It is our preceptor.”
“Has the Blessed One allowed a carriage?”
“He has.”

The group of six monks said, “The Blessed One thinks the Śākyans are so soft that they cannot travel by foot, and so the Blessed One has allowed a carriage.” With this in mind, they prepared carriages of gold and silver and lapis lazuli and variegated crystal, polished like a lotus. While riding in such a carriage in the center of the road, they stuck out their arms and hailed, “Be well!” to brahmins and householders.
The brahmins and householders asked, “Noble ones, has the Blessed One allowed these carriages?”
“He has.”
They said, “Noble ones, you ascetics are obsessed with sense pleasures.”

Other monks reported what they said to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One reflected, “The problem that has arisen here is that the monks travel by carriage. That being the case, a monk should not travel by carriage.”

When that in turn became a problem, the Blessed One said, “There are two situations in which a monk may travel by carriage: when he is weak and infirm from old age, and when he is ill and unable to tolerate exertion.”

div.head: Swimming
div.p: Some monks left to wander the countryside. Carrying their robes and begging bowls, they crossed a river, getting as far as the middle until they could go no farther. Monks reported this incident to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said, “That being the case, I allow that a monk may learn how to swim.”

After the Blessed One had said that a monk may learn how to swim, the group of six monks swam to and fro across the great river Ajiravatī. When some female cowherds arrived, the group of six saw them and said, “Sisters, come here! We will save you!”
In the process of saving them, the group of six touched their hands and arms and after saving them, they said, “Sisters, come back here! We’ll save you again!”
The women chastised, rebuked, and criticized them, saying, “Noble ones, since you have touched our hands and arms in a way that not even our husbands touch us, why should we come back?”

Monks reported this matter to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said,“Said,” following the Sanskrit (bhagavān āha) rather than the Tibetan bcom ldan ’das kyis dgongs pa or “the Blessed One reflected.” In the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, the Buddha is often described as first reflecting on a situation and then announcing a new rule to address the situation. “The problem that has arisen here is that the monks have been touching women. That being the case, a monk may not touch a woman. If he does so, he will be guilty of a breach.”

After the Blessed One said that a monk may not touch a woman, city folk who enjoyed gardens visited a garden. One of the women went down into the river, slipped, and was carried off by the water. Her husband, who went in search of her, saw the monks and asked, “Noble ones, have you seen my wife who has been carried off by the water?”
“We saw her. The water has carried her over there.”
“Have you been standing here all day looking at the water while she’s almost drowning over there?”
Monks reported this incident to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said, “She should be removed. Others may say, ‘Let me go!’ If they are able to swim, they should be released.”
The monks covered the other women, who lay motionless and face down, under mounds of sand and left, but then the corpses were eaten by jackals.
“A monk should not leave without disposing of a female corpse.”

When the monks grew desirous while disposing of female corpses, the Blessed One said, “Sit down elsewhere, and those who recite should recite while those who meditate should direct their attention. If a monk becomes worried about lunch, he should entrust the female corpses to a cowherd or shepherd in the vicinity and then depart.”

div.head: Cow’s Tail and Inappropriate Motifs
div.p: The group of six monks grabbed the tail of a householder’s cow and swam to and fro [in the river] until the cow ran out of milk.
The householder asked the cowherd, “Why is there no milk today?”
He replied, “The noble ones, the group of six monks…,” etc., as above, up to chastised, rebuked, and criticized them.
The Blessed One said, “That being the case, a monk may not swim while holding on to a cow’s tail.”

After the Blessed One said that a monk may not swim while holding on to a cow’s tail, some monks were crossing a river holding on to an elephant’s tail when they decided they did not want to cross. The Blessed One said, “A monk may hold the tails of any of five types of animals with tails when crossing: a well-bred elephant, a well-bred horse, a prize bull, a buffalo, or a yak. However, a monk should swim with a bladder.”“Bladder” (Tib. rkyal pa). In his edition of the Sanskrit manuscripts from Gilgit, Dutt (1950) reads Skt. bhṛṣi. See though Bhattacarya’s discussion of the Vedic dṛti or “leather container” and its later meanings of “inflated skin bag” or “bladder and bellows.” Thanks to Shayne Clarke for this reference.

After the Blessed One said that a bladder should be used when swimming, the group of six monks made bladders, polished like a lotus, with colorful motifs depicting women and men making love. Brahmins and householders saw them and said, “Noble ones, what is this?”
They replied, “The Blessed One has allowed a bladder.”
“Noble ones, you ascetics are obsessed with sense pleasures.”

Monks reported these disparaging words to the Blessed One‍—and the Blessed One said, “An ascetic may not keep bladders, polished like a lotus, with colorful motifs depicting women and men making love. He may, however, keep one of two types of bladder: one for dye“Dye” (Tib. rtsis bskus pa; Skt. kāṣāya); that is, dye used to dye cloth when it is to be made into robes. and one to be used as a rudder.”“Rudder” (Tib. rkyal skya; Skt. tāraṇika).

div.head: Wooden Sandals
div.p: The householder Anāthapiṇḍada built a garden. When monks wearing wooden sandals arrived to listen to the Dharma, their wooden sandals scraped the polished floor and ruined it.

The householder Anāthapiṇḍada, who had gotten up early to go and bow at the feet of the Blessed One, saw what had happened and asked the monks, “Noble ones, oh my, did King Prasenajit, together with the four branches of the army, set up camp for the night here?”
“It was not the king,” they replied, “but some monks who arrived wearing wooden sandals. They spoiled this.”

Monks reported their disparaging words to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One reflected, “The problem that has arisen here is that the monks wear wooden sandals. That being the case, a monk may not wear wooden sandals.”

After the Blessed One said that a monk may not wear wooden sandals, some monks pledged to spend the rains retreat in an apartment. When their sandals got wet with mud, monks reported this incident to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said, “A monk may keep wooden sandals to wear inside an apartment.”
When faithful brahmins and householders offered wooden sandals to the saṅgha of monks, the monks did not accept them.

The brahmins and householders said, “Nobles ones, until the Blessed Buddha appeared in the world, we were patrons of the tīrthikas…” and so forth, up to “Accept [the gifts].”The text refers to a formula found in full in The Chapter on Minor Matters of Monastic Discipline, which reads, “Noble ones, until the Blessed Buddha appeared in the world, we were patrons of the tīrthikas. Now that the Blessed Buddha has appeared in the world, we are your patrons. If you do not accept [our gifts], you leave us without provisions on the path of virtue from this life to the next, do you not? Accept [the gifts].” See Toh 6, F.205.b: ’phags pa dag gang gi tshe sangs rgyas bcom ldan ’das ’jig rten du ma byung ba de’i tshe ni mu stegs can rnams sbyin gnas lags/bcom ldan ’das ’jig rten du byung na da ltar khyed sbyin gnas lags na/ gal te khyed mi bzhes na ci bdag cag ’jig rten ’di nas ’jig rten pha rol tu dge ba’i lam rgyags ma mchis par mchi’am.

The Blessed One said, “[Wooden sandals] should be accepted and placed in the saṅgha’s toilet or privy.”The “toilet” (Tib. snam phyi sa; Skt. varcaskuṭi) is for defecating, while the “privy” (Tib. chab khung; Skt. prasrāvakuṭi) is for urination. [B22]

div.head: Summary 2
div.p: A summary:

l.hi: Bamboo leaves; Śroṇa; Allow to protect; Likewise, lace up crosswise; Toe cover, and heel cover.

div.head: Bamboo Leaves
div.p: After the Blessed One said that a monk may not keep wooden sandals, they wore bamboo-leaf sandals.The content of the text continues from the previous section despite the summary break. This may explain the absence of a nidāna or narrative “setting,” which ordinarily comes immediately after an uddāna or “summary” in The Chapters on Monastic Discipline and The Chapter on Minor Matters of Monastic Discipline. When that in turn became a problem, the Blessed One said, “Bamboo-leaf sandals may not be worn.”
When the monks wore muñja grass sandals and that in turn became a problem, the Blessed One said, “Muñja grass sandals may not be worn.”
When they wore rope sandals and that in turn became a problem, the Blessed One said, “Rope sandals may not be worn.”

When the monks developed rheumatismHere, our identification of Skt. vātaśonita (Tib. dreg gyis btab pa) with “rheumatism” is based on Sanskrit and Tibetan dictionaries. The “abscesses” (shu ba) mentioned in the Tibetan translation could correspond to tophi, masses of uric acid crystals that burst open under pressure, which develop in people with tophaceous gout, a type of rheumatism. Gout, however, is a condition associated with overnutrition and so is unlikely to have been widespread in ancient India. because their leather sandals remained wet when they were inundated.Yijing’s Chinese translation states that monks developed an illness or pain in their hips, which caused them so much pain that they would always have to sprinkle water, which left their leather sandals wet; see Yijing’s Taishō 1447, 1055c10–11: liang bi you bing kutong suo bi dang yi shuilin pi lu zong lan 兩髀有病苦痛所逼當以水淋皮履總爛. Monks reported the matter to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said, “A monk who develops rheumatism may wear rope sandals.”

div.head: Śroṇa
div.p: When Śroṇa Koṭīviṃśa went forth, he had gold-colored hairs four finger-widths long on the soles of his feet. On seeing this, the group of six said, “Nanda, Upananda! How will this ball of fresh butter make single-pointed effort now that he has gone forth in the Teaching?”

On hearing what they said, Śroṇa’s pride was piqued. He went to the venerable Ānanda and asked him, “Honorable Ānanda, how, in the Blessed One’s telling, does a person with single-pointed effort remain in absorption on the Teaching?”

“Venerable Śroṇa, it is attained by walking.”Here, the phrase “by walking” (Tib. ’chag pas; Skt. caṃkramya) refers to walking meditation, as mentioned in The Chapter on the Restoration Rite at 1.57, where the Buddha advises monks who feel sleepy while doing seated meditation to do walk a bit to dispel the torpor.

Śroṇa went to the Śitavana cemetery, where he practiced walking,“Practiced walking” (Tib. ’chags bcas so; Skt. caṃkrame ’dhiṣṭhitaḥ). and the walking wore down the gold-colored hairs, which had been four finger-widths long. Moreover, his skin cracked and then started to bleed. As he went from one end of the walkway to the other, crows drank his blood at both ends.

It is natural that blessed buddhas who live and remain in the world need, from time to time, to swim in order to cross a river, and so forth.The Skt. and Tib. texts direct the reader to fill in material, though in this case it is not obvious where in The Chapters on Monastic Discipline the full citation can be found. Nor is it obvious why the text refers to crossing a river, which is taken up under the heading “Swimming,” below: Tib. chos nyid kyis sangs rgyas bcom ldan ’das rnams bzhugs shing ’tsho la gzhes pa ni dus dus su chu bor rgyu ba spyod do // zhes bya ba’i bar du rgyas par bya ste / skabs ’dir ni; Skt. dharmatā khalu buddhā bhagavanto jīvantas tiṣṭhanto dhriyamāṇā yāpayantaḥ kālena kālaṃ nadīcārikāṃ caranti vistareṇa yāvad evāsmiṃs tv arthe. On this occasion, the Blessed Buddha came to the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīviṃśa’s monastery and saw his walkway. Because blessed buddhas ask questions even though they know the answers, upon seeing him, the Blessed Buddha, although he knew, inquired of the venerable Ānanda, “Ānanda, which monk does this walkway for one-pointed effort belong to?”
“It is Śroṇa Koṭīviṃśa’s.”
“Ānanda, that being the case, I allow that Śroṇa Koṭīviṃśa may affix a single lining to his sandals, but not a second layer, nor a third layer. If it breaks down, it can be patched and kept.”

The venerable Ānanda then went to the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīviṃśa and said to the venerable Śroṇa Koṭīviṃśa, “Venerable one, you should know: The Teacher has allowed you to affix a single lining in your sandals, but not a second layer, nor a third layer…and so forth, as before.”
“Honorable Ānanda, is this allowance for the whole saṅgha, or only for me?”
“Only for you.”

“Honorable Ānanda, the Teacher’s command is not to be disregarded. However, if I were to keep them, it would give my fellow brahmacārins grounds to say to me, ‘Ah, Śrona Koṭiviṃśa has received a privileged position since going forth. Look at how the one who abandoned the unique country of Campā with its host of seven elephants and went forth, now clings to sandals with a single lining!’ Be that as it may, if the Blessed One would concur with me and make the allowance for the whole saṅgha, I would keep them as well.”

The venerable Ānanda reported Śroṇa’s words to the Blessed One at length, and the Blessed One said, “That being the case, thanks to Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa, I allow that the whole saṅgha may keep sandals with a single lining affixed, but not a second layer, nor a third layer…and so forth, as before.”

div.head: Allow to Protect
div.p: A latecomer monk“Latecomer” (Tib. rgan zhugs; Skt. mahalla and mahallaka). “Latecomer” refers to those who join the saṅgha late in life, generally in retirement from a household life. See Śīlapālita (Toh 4115, F.43.b): rgan zhugs zhes bya ba ni na tshod rgas nas rab tu byung ba’o. It is not a technical term for a monastic station in the way novice, monk, and nun are, as they each require a profession. There is no Chinese translation for this portion of the present text, but in The Chapter on Going Forth (Taishō 1444), Yijing translates the term you nian lao 有年老. walked in front of the Blessed One while wearing sandals. The Blessed One said to that monk, “Monk, go away. Do not stand in front of me.”

Then the Blessed One said to the monks, “Monks, if the disciples of a teacher who is entangled in all of the world’s luxuries do not swiftly and easily resort to ways of life leading to downfall, how much more so those of one who is detached from all of the world’s luxuries?”Here, the Buddha allows that a teacher preoccupied with wealth may have students who do not emulate his avarice and therefore may avoid the pitfalls that the teacher falls into, i.e., the miserable realms of existence. But, since it will be all the easier for the students of a teacher who exhibits restraint toward wealth and material goods to avoid those pitfalls, the Buddha goes on to ban the keeping of lined sandals.
The Blessed One considered, “The problem that has arisen here is that the monks wear sandals. That being the case, a monk may not wear sandals.”

After the Blessed One said that a monk may not wear sandals, a dancer who had gone forth had a broken water jug. So, after he had washed his feet at the foot-washing building, he filled up the jug with water and walked away in the peacock’s gait.The “peacock’s gait” (Tib. rma bya’i ’gros; Skt. mayūragati) is the name of a meter but the Skt. gati also means “gait, (dance) step.” Here, the monks presumably adopts this gait so as not to spill the water he is carrying. The group of six monks saw him and said, “Venerable ones, the monk dances without musical accompaniment. Let us play him some music!” and they began to make music with their mouths.

The other monks asked them, “Why are you being so frivolous?”The translation here follows the Sanskrit (kim auddhatyam kuruta). The Tibetan ci ma grangs pa byed dam may be rendered as, “Are you acting without account?”
They replied, “What is frivolous about this? Have you not seen that monk dancing without music?”

Monks reported this incident to the Blessed One. Because blessed ones ask questions even though they know the answers, the Buddha, although he knew the answer, asked that monk, “Monk, what was your intention in acting like that?”
“Honorable One, I was acting to protect the dwellings.”

“Since this monk was acting to protect the dwellings, he has incurred no offense. That being the case, I allow that sandals with a single lining may be worn to protect the dwellings, but not a second lining, nor a third. If it breaks down, it can be patched and kept.”

After the Blessed One said that sandals with a single lining may be worn, a householder became devoted to an alms seeker.That is, a monk who relies only on alms and does not accept invitations to meals offered by patrons. Living only by alms is one of the thirteen “practices of austerity,” on which see Roach 2020, pp. 111–14. The latter got up early and entered Śrāvastī on his alms round, without any sandals. The householder saw him without sandals and said, “Does the noble one not have sandals? Come! Let us go to see the shoemaker, and I will see that he gives you sandals.”
He went to the shoemaker and said to him, “Ah, my good man! Give a pair of sandals to the noble one here.” After giving his instructions, he left.
At that time, sandals with single linings were made in two cases: for monks and for the dead. Although that monk went to see the shoemaker time and time again, he did not give him the sandals.
Later, the householder saw the monk again without any sandals, and on seeing him he said, “Noble one, did the shoemaker not give you sandals?”
“Householder, have you not heard that telling the truth is a rarity in a shoemaker? I went again and again, but he did not give them to me.”
“Noble one, come! Let us go to my house,” replied the householder.
He took the monk home and, after the monk had his fill of nourishing food, the householder gave him a pair of sandals with many linings.

The monk said, “Householder, the Blessed One has not allowed these.”Here and in the following five instances of this phrase, the Tibetan translation of The Chapter on Leather translates the Skt. prefix anu- with the Tib. prefix rjes su (Skt. bhagavata nānujñātam; Tib. bcom ldan ’das kyis rjes su ma gnang ngo). In The Chapters on Monastic Discipline and the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya more broadly, this formula is more commonly seen in Tibetan without the prefix (e.g., Tib. bcom ldan ’das kyis ma gnang ngo). The Chapter on Going Forth (Toh 1-1, 1.659) distinguishes between a “prescription” (Skt. prajñapti; Tib. bcas pa) and a “supplementary prescription” (Skt. anuprajñapti; Tib. rjes su bcas pa). Here in The Chapter on Leather, however, the Sanskrit prefix anu- does not appear to carry any additional semantic value.
He replied, “Noble one, take them. Go and make them into sandals with a single lining; then you may wear them.”
The monk took them and returned to the monastery. Taking the sandals and a knife, he sat down at the foot of a tree, where he started to take them apart.
The Blessed One came by that place and asked, “Monk, what are you doing?”
“After the Blessed One banned sandals with many linings, I got these ones with multiple linings. I’ll make them into single-lined sandals and wear them.”

Then the Blessed One reflected, “Oh dear, see how devoted brahmins and householders give sandals with many linings to my disciples! That being the case, I allow that monks may wear sandals with many linings if the sandals were used by a householder.”

The venerable Upāli asked, and so forth, up to,It is unclear what text these instructions intend to be supplied here. No further content is necessary for coherence. “Honorable One, the Blessed One has said that monks may wear sandals with many linings if the sandals were used by a householder. Honorable One, how should one interpret ‘used by a householder’?”
“Upāli, if they have been worn for at least seven or eight paces, they meet the requirement.”

div.head: Lace up Crosswise, Toe Cover, and Heel Cover
The Noble Ones Steal Sandals
div.p: The setting was at Vaiśālī.Vaiśālī was the capital of the Vṛji/Vaji/Vajji (Tib. spong byed; Pali Vajji) republic, home to the Malla and Licchavi people and one of the sixteen mahājanapada of ancient India (Edgerton vaji, p. 467, col. 1). The Vaji Republic was Magadha’s immediate neighbor to the north and northeast, their territory including a swath of the Himālayan foothills. The mahājanapada of Aśmaka, by contrast, where Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa’s story takes place, was to the far south of Magadha, around the Godāvarī River in modern-day Maharasthra. At that time they made the following sorts of sandals in Vaiśālī: creaking sandals,Tib. thrig thrig; Skt. hinihināyamānā, which BHSD, p. 620, suggests is onomatopoeic. tinkling sandals,Tib. tshig tshig; Skt. kiṇikiṇāyamānā, which BHSD, p. 183, suggests is onomatopoetic. sparkling sandals,Tib. chem chem; no equivalent in Skt. Or this might also be onomatopoeic, reflecting a loud sound; see WTS, pp. 271–72. ram’s horn sandals,Tib. lug ru; Skt. meṇḍaviṣāṇikā. sandals like a bodhi leaf,Tib. byang chub kyi shing gi lo ma lta bu; Skt. bodhi­paṭapatrakā. multicolored sandals,Tib. khra bo; Skt. citropacitrā. and sandals that cost five coins. The group of six monks saw someone wearing some sandals, and they pressed down on the heel of his sandals with their big toes and pushed him by the nape of his neck. Saying, “By your giving this donation, may you attain mental adornment,Although the group of six constantly flout Buddhist doctrine, they are well versed in it and frequently quote it for their own ends. The four attainments they list here flow from practicing “relinquishment,” i.e., donating possessions to others. For a systematic presentation of “relinquishment” and the other “requisites” on the spiritual path of the śrāvaka, see the Śrāvakabhūmi, summarized and discussed by Kragh 2013, pp. 110–18. the mental requirements, the requisites for practicing yoga, and the ultimate goal‍—buddhahood!” they took the sandals. News spread everywhere: “The noble ones steal shoes like that.”

They stole such sandals from an older noblewoman. The son-in-law came to her, and after giving her a pair of sandals, instructed her, “Since the noble ones steal sandals, be alert, and by all means, do not allow them to steal these!”

Some time later, the son-in-law saw an alms seeker entering Vaiśālī on his alms round. The man went into a house, and the monk entered it too. The man left the house and entered another house. The monk then entered that one too. The man said, “Noble one, why are you following in my footsteps, unless perhaps you wish to steal these sandals?”
“My friend, I will not steal your sandals; rather, I have come here for alms.”
The man said, “Noble one, whether you steal them or not, everywhere it is said that you noble ones steal sandals.”

Monks reported what people were saying to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One reflected, “The problem that has arisen here is that the monks wear creaking sandals, and so forth in full from above.The full text above (1.294) reads “creaking sandals, tinkling sandals, sparkling sandals, ram’s horn sandals, sandals like a bodhi leaf, multicolored sandals, and sandals that cost five coins.” A monk should not wear such sandals. If a monk wears such sandals, he will be guilty of a breach.”

div.head: Lace Up Crosswise
div.p: The setting was at Śrāvastī. At that time, when brahmins and householders saw that the monks’ sandals caused sores on the tops of their feet, they said, “It is your sandals that are causing sores on the tops of your feet. Why do you not wear sandals that lace up crosswise?”
“The Blessed One has not allowed them.”
Monks reported this incident to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said, “That being the case, I allow that sandals laced up crosswise may be worn.”

div.head: Toe Cover
div.p: Monks developed sores on their big toes while climbing Vulture Peak Mountain. Brahmins and householders saw them and said, “You developed sores on your big toes from climbing Vulture Peak Mountain. Why do you not wear sandals with toe covers?”The translation “with toe cover” follows the Skt. puṭāpuṭī, which suggests a small piece of cloth, in preference to the Tibetan (rtse mo sbu bu can), which seems to read “with a hole at the toe.”
“The Blessed One has not allowed them.”
Monks reported this incident to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said, “That being the case, I allow that a monk may wear sandals with toe covers.”

div.head: Heel Cover
div.p: When brahmins and householders saw that monks developed sores on their heels when coming down from Vulture Peak Mountain, they asked, “Noble ones, how did you develop sores on your heels?”
“Coming down from Vulture Peak Mountain.”
“Why do you not wear sandals with heel covers?”
“The Blessed One has not allowed them.”
Monks reported this incident to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said, “That being the case, I allow that a monk may wear sandals with heel covers.”

div.head: Summary 3
div.p: A summary:

lg.l: Ankle boots and boots; Buskins in snowy regions; Allowing bearskin; Awls, straps, and knives.

div.head: Ankle Boots and Boots
div.p: The setting was at Śrāvastī. When brahmins and householders saw that the new grass cut the monks’ feet, they asked, “Noble ones, what has caused the cuts on your feet?”
“New grass.”

“Why did not you wear ankle boots?”Tib. lham mgul chad; Skt. nālāmbujā. The Tibetan suggests a boot, i.e., in which the “neck” (Tib. mgul) has been “cut” (Tib. chad). The Skt. literally means “hollow stalked lotus,” apparently in reference to the hollow bootleg.
“The Blessed One has not allowed them.”
Monks reported this incident to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said, “That being the case, I allow that ankle boots may be worn.”

When the new grass grew tall, the monks’ legs were cut even worse.The following few lines are not found in the Skt. MSS. On seeing this, brahmins and householders asked, “How did your legs get cut even worse?”
They replied, “When the new grass grew tall, it cut our legs even worse.”
“Noble ones, why did not you wear boots?”Tib. lham yu’u thung, or lham yu thung, lit. “short boot”; Skt. muṇḍapūlā.
“The Blessed One has not allowed them.”
Monks reported this incident to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said, “That being the case, I allow that boots may be worn.”

div.head: Buskins in Snowy Regions
div.p: As the deluded Virūḍhaka was killing the Śākyans of Kapilavastu,For much of the Buddha’s teaching career, Virūḍhaka ruled Kośala, the mahājanapada that neighbored the Śākyan homeland of Kapilavastu, and its capital at Śrāvastī. Virūḍhaka was the son of King Prasenajit and a Śākyan maidservant. He is said to have nursed a lifelong grievance against the Śākyans and, after his father’s death, launched a savage attack on them, as described here. See The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism (2014, p. 980). some of them fled west to the Northern Route,The Tib. reads nub phyogs or “west” while the Skt. reads uttarāpatha or “Northern Route.” The lands that made up the Northern Route lay due west of Kapilavastu, just north of the Nepal border, which perhaps explains the discrepancy between the Skt. and Tib., which we have combined in our translation. while some went as far as Nepal.“Nepal”: Tib. bal yul; Skt. naivāla (i.e. Naipāla).
Merchants from Śrāvastī brought their wares and came to Nepal. When the Śākyans saw them, they said, “Gentlemen, from where have you come today?”This portion is not found in the Sanskrit.
The merchants replied, “From Śrāvastī.”
“If our beloved kin, the noble Ānanda, is there, would you give the noble Ānanda the message that, upon coming here, you spoke with us?”
The merchants said, “WeReading with Stok Palace bdag gis in preference to Degé bdag gi. will go to the noble Ānanda and deliver the message.”
Taking goods to be traded, they arrived in Śrāvastī and said to the noble Ānanda, “Noble Ānanda, your beloved kin are in Nepal, and they wish to see you.”

The venerable Ānanda, who had not seen his beloved kin,The translation combines the Tibetan gnyen la gdung ba yin pas or “who longed for his kin” with the Skt. āryānando jñātivatsalo nāsmākam avalokayatīti / yāvat tais tasyārocitam. went to Nepal to see them. His hands and feet cracked from the cold, and the monks said to him, “Venerable Ānanda, why do your hands and feet look like torn lotus leaves?”
“On account of the cold,” he replied.
“Since your kin wear buskins,“Buskins” (Tib. lham sgro gu can; Skt. pūlā or maṇḍapūlā). A buskin is a calf- or knee-high boot. why do you not wear them?”
“The Blessed One has not allowed them.”
Other monks reported this incident to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said, “That being the case, I allow that buskins may be worn in snowy regions.”
The venerable Upāli asked the Blessed Buddha, “Honorable One, the Blessed One has said that buskins may be worn in snowy regions. Honorable One, what is a ‘snowy region’?”
“Upāli, it is a place where water freezes in the jug.”

A hunter became devoted to a monk and offered him a bearskin that he had obtained, but the monk did not accept it. The hunter followed the monk and said, “Noble one, I am offering you a bearskin that I have obtained, so please accept it!” That monk still did not accept it.

The Blessed One then too came to that place. Since blessed buddhas ask questions even though they know the answers, asking although he knew the answer, the Blessed Buddha inquired of Ānanda, “Ānanda, why is that hunter over there following this monk?”
“Honorable One, when that hunter obtained a bearskin he presented it to this monk, but the monk did not accept it, so the hunter came here following this monk.”

The Blessed One said, “Ānanda, a hunter with faith is a rarity. If he comes across a hundred or a thousand beings, he kills them. So therefore, if a hunter gives a bearskin, it may be accepted. Having been accepted, it should be spread out at the door of the perfumed chamberThe “perfumed chamber” (Tib. dri gtsang khang; Skt. gandhakuṭi) is a shrine room, generally containing a statue or painting of the Buddha, and therefore a place that lay people might visit when paying homage at the monastery. For more, see Schopen 2015. or underfoot, where it may benefit the eye.”“Benefit the eye” (Tib. mig la phan no; Skt. cākṣuṣyaṃ svasti). Yijing’s Chinese translation attributes a healing benefit to seeing these objects, thus explaining their placement in semipublic places; see Taishō 1447 1057b10–11: 能令明眼并療痔疾.

div.head: Awls, straps, and knives
div.p: Another monk’s sandals were broken. He went to a shoemaker and when he arrived, he said, “My friend, stitch up my broken sandals and give them back to me!”
The shoemaker thought, “Because the ascetic heirs of the Śākyan seek services without paying for them, I will procrastinate, and he will change his mind.”
Thinking in this way, he said, “Noble one, I am busy, so come back tomorrow!”
Although that monk went back to the shoemaker time and time again, the latter procrastinated.
When his fellow monks saw that, they asked him, “Venerable one, is that the house that supplies you food“Supplies” or “prepares” food (Tib. zas sbyor; Skt. none). or the house of your kin?”

He replied, “It is neither the house that supplies me food nor the house of my kin. Rather, my sandals were broken, so I said to the shoemaker, ‘Stitch them up and give them back to me!’ but he has procrastinated.”

One of them said to him, “Have you not heard that truth-telling is a rarity in a shoemaker? However, since I have the skill, if the Blessed One allows it, I will stitch them up and give them back to you.”

Monks reported this incident to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said, “If a monk has the skill, he may do the stitching while seated in a secluded spot where another will not lose faith if they see him.”The Sanskrit does not contain this last phrase, “where another will not lose faith if they see him” (Tib. gzhan gyis mthong na mi dad par mi ’gyur ba).
The first monk went to see his fellow monk and said to him, “Since the Blessed One has allowed it, stitch them up!”
The other monk replied, “What do you think it should be stitched with?”
“Well, for that you need an awl, a knife, and a strap.”

Monks reported this incident to the Blessed One, and the Blessed One said, “If a monk has the skill, he may keep an awl and a strap. He should not carry a knife.”The Skt. Chapter on Leather allows all three: bhagavān āha / tasmād anujānāmi bhikṣuṇā ārā śastrakaṃ baddhikā ca dhārayitavyāḥ. In his digest of this, though, Guṇaprabha says that knives are disallowed, which is also stated in the Tibetan translation of Chapter on Leather (Toh 4117, F.74.b): de’i ched du smyung bu dang ko ba bcang bar bya’o//dra gri ni ma yin. The original Sanskrit of Guṇaprabha’s sūtra reads dhārayet tadartham ārāṃ vardhrañ ca na śastrīm.

note.p: The Sanskrit term pāradārika means one who has intercourse with “another’s wife.”
A river at Śrāvastī.
One of the ascetic practices known as dhūtaguṇas.
One of several types of footwear allowed for Buddhist monks in The Chapter on Leather.

One of twelve literary genres found in the Buddhist canon, avadānas (meaning “heroic acts” or “glorious exploits”) relate the past life actions which have culminated in a person’s present life attainments.
Monks who know how to make and repair leather footwear are allowed to keep an awl, a strap, and, according to some sources, a knife to assist them in these tasks.
A wealthy householder, father of Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa.
A denigratory way to refer to a Buddhist monk.
Site of the Kalandaka Sanctuary.
One of several types of footwear described in The Chapter on Leather.
One of several types of furniture allowed for Buddhist monks mentioned in The Chapter on Leather.

term.hi: byang chub kyi shing gi lo ma

note.p: Sandals that resemble a bodhi leaf in shape. One of several types of footwear prohibited in The Chapter on Leather, along with creaking sandals, tinkling sandals, sparkling sandals, ram’s horn sandals, multicolored sandals, and sandals that cost five coins.
One of several types of footwear allowed for Buddhist monks in The Chapter on Leather.

The term “breach” does not uniquely correspond to any of the five types of offense a monk should avoid. Instead, context determines which class of offense any specific breach belongs to. See Clarke 2021 p, 71 n. 80.
A calf- or thigh-high boot allowed for monks living in snowy regions. One of several types of footwear allowed for Buddhist monks in The Chapter on Leather.
Ancient capital of Aṅga.
One of several types of leather or hide used in ground-spreads and ground-spread covers in the frontier country of Aśmaka.

Perhaps onomatopoeic. Monks are not allowed to wear creaking sandals. One of several types of footwear prohibited in The Chapter on Leather, along with tinkling sandals, sparkling sandals, ram’s horn sandals, sandals like a bodhi leaf, multicolored sandals, and sandals that cost five coins.
One of several types of leather or hide used in ground-spreads and ground-spread covers in the frontier country of Aśmaka.

In the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, the term dhyāna is used in a general sense to mean “meditation.” Note, however, that in Buddhist literature dhyāna often refers to specific meditative states involving increasing detachment from both sensory and mental objects.
An attendant to Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa, whose name means “servant.”

term.hi: tshangs pa mtshungs par spyod pa

note.p: In a Buddhist context, the term brahman or “holy” refers to nirvāṇa, and hence a Buddhist brahmacārin is a monk engaged in or living the “holy life.” The “holy life” is generally understood to mean a “life of celibacy” which, in Buddhist usage, implies also a life that leads to nirvāṇa. Here, a “fellow brahmacārin” would thus be a fellow monk.

term.hi: dgongs pa mngon du bzhag ste

note.p: To collect one’s attention and direct it to meditation.

The Mūlasarvāstivādin tradition grouped the Buddha’s early sūtra discourses into four divisions, or āgama (Tib. mdo sde’i lung sde bzhi): the Dīrghāgama (Tib. lung ring po), the Madhyamāgama (Tib. lung bar ma), the Ekottarikāgama (Tib. lung gcig las ’phros pa), and the Saṃyuktāgama (Tib. lung dag ldan/yang dar par ldan pa’i lung). They are more familiar to many English-speaking Buddhists through the translations of their Pali correlates: the Dīgha Nikāya, Majjhima Nikāya, Aṅguttara Nikāya, and the Samyutta Nikāya, for which see the Wisdom Publications titles: The Long Discourses of the Buddha, The Middle-Length Discourses of the Buddha, The Numerical Discourses of the Buddha, and The Connected Discourses of the Buddha, respectively.

term.hi: rdo can zhes bya ba’i yul gyi mtha’

note.p: The Sanskrit Chapter on Leather gives this location first in the probably erroneous form aśmakana­garāntaka (Tib. F.251.b rdo can zhes bya ba’i yul gyi mtha’ na), or “border town of Aśma.” In the second case, the probably correct Sanskrit reads aśmāparāntakeṣu jana­padeṣu or “in the frontier land of Aśma” (the Tibetan translation is identical to the previous instance: rdo can zhes bya ba’i yul gyi mtha’ na). According to Edgerton, the Skt. aparāntaka (p. 44, col. 2) can refer specifically to the country of Aparānta or more generally to the western border (i.e., in present-day Maharashtra). In his translation of the parallel passages in the Divyāvadāna, Rotman (2008: p. 392, n. 85) gives “region of Aśmāparāntaka,” which he explains to be a conjunct of Aparānta (as in “the country of…”) and Aśma (a.k.a. Aśmaka, Aśvaka, Pali Assaka, the latter of which is also in present-day Maharashtra). The Tibetan translation supports Rotman’s suggestion to take Aśma to be the name of a specific region in the western lands, rendered in Tibetan as rdo can. Thus, here, we have elected to translate this phrase in both instances as “the frontier country of Aśmaka.”
Third stage on the path to becoming an arhat.
Second stage on the path to becoming an arhat.
First stage on the path to becoming an arhat.

This is one of many related terms for an assembly hall that appear in the Kangyur and Tengyur, such as (1) “meditation residence” or “meditation hall” (Tib. spong khang; Skt. prahāṇaśāla), (2) “multistoried structure” (Tib. khang pa rtseg ma’i khyams; Skt. kūṭāgāraśālā), (3) “temple” or “hall” (various Tib., e.g. khyams and khang bzangs; Skt. prāsāda), (4) “steps” (Tib. bang rim; Skt. pariṣaṇḍā), and (5) “courtyard” or “terrace” (Tib. ’khor gyi khyams; Skt. maṇḍalavāṭa).
One of several types of leather or hide used in ground-spreads and ground-spread covers in the frontier country of Aśmaka.

In his Chinese translation of The Chapter on Leather, Yijing (T 1447, 1052b12) gives a single term, wo ju 臥具 where the Sanskrit from Gilgit gives both āstaraṇa or “ground-spread” and pratyāstaraṇa or “ground-spread cover.” Ground-spreads and ground-spread covers were laid out on the ground or on furniture so that monks could sit or sleep comfortably and without damaging the community’s furniture.
A group of monks led by Nanda and Upananda who are consistently portrayed as indulgent and attached to material comforts. See the introduction to The Chapter on Leather.

term.hi: sum bcu rtsa gsum pa’i gnas

note.p: In a Buddhist context, the term brahman or “holy” refers to nirvāṇa, and hence, for a Buddhist, the “holy life” means living a life conducive to nirvāṇa, generally implying celibacy.
The name of an island or group of islands where precious stones and gems were extraordinarily plentiful and easily obtained.

This site derives its names from the birds whose chirping awoke King Bimbisāra and saved him from the bite of a deadly snake. In gratitude, the king forbade harming the birds in this park. The Sanskrit Chapter on a Schism in the Saṅgha identifies the kalandaka as a bird in its telling of the site’s origins (Toh 1-17, F.77.b–78.a). There, the Sanskrit glosses the site’s name as “the winged ones known as ‘kalandaka’ ”: kalandakā nāma pakṣiṇaḥ) while the Tibetan appends the word for “bird” (Tib. bya) to a transliteration of kalandaka, in effect designating the kalandaka a type of bird (Toh 1-17 F.77.b): bya ka lan ka zhes bya ba de rnams kyis). The Skt. pakṣin, “winged one,” is a common poetic synonym for “bird.”
The Śākyan capital, home of the Bodhisattva before his renunciation.
Prasenajit’s kingdom and later Virūḍhaka’s.
Latin: Dolichos uniflorus (MW), synonymous with Macrotyloma uniflorum or “horse gram.”
Late October/Early November.
King of Vārāṇasī during the time of the Buddha Kāśyapa.
One of several types of footwear allowed for Buddhist monks in The Chapter on Leather.

term.hi: kA tyA ya na chen po

note.p: The Sanskrit terms śayyā and śayanāsana were rendered into Tibetan with both mal cha and mal stan.
In The Chapter on Leather, the Sanskrit term vihāra is used to refer to individual buildings in a monastic compound.
One of several types of footwear allowed for Buddhist monks in The Chapter on Leather.
In the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, mātṛkā (Tib. ma mo, Eng. “mother”) is frequently used as a name for the Basket of Abhidharma.
Although the name “Nepal” derives from Naivāla (also given as Naipāla and Nepāla), the ancient Nepāla would probably not extend beyond the Kathmandu Valley.
One type of offense Buddhist monks must avoid. This type of offense stems from the monk possessing material goods, such as cloth, in excess of allowance.
A lost verse text, which was possibly a Mūlasarvāstivādin counterpart of the Pārāyanavagga of the Suttanipāta in the Pāli canon and thought to be included in the Kṣudrakavastu.

term.hi: rgyal rgyal byed kyi tshal mgon med zas sbyin gyi kun dga’ ra ba

note.p: A forest to the east, beyond which the Buddha designated “border regions.”
A town to the east, adjacent to the border forest of Puṇḍrakakṣa.
An attendant to Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa, whose name means “protector.”

Monks are not allowed to wear ram’s horn. One of several types of footwear prohibited in The Chapter on Leather, along with creaking sandals, tinkling sandals, sparkling sandals, sandals like a bodhi leaf, multicolored sandals, and sandals that cost five coins.
One of several types of footwear allowed for Buddhist monks in The Chapter on Leather.
One of several types of footwear allowed for Buddhist monks in The Chapter on Leather.
One of several types of furniture allowed for Buddhist monks.
One of several types of material used in ground-spreads and ground-spread covers in the Buddhist heartland of ancient India.
One of several types of material used in ground-spreads and ground-spread covers in the Buddhist heartland of ancient India.
One of several types of material used in ground-spreads and ground-spread covers in the Buddhist heartland of ancient India.
One of several types of material used in ground-spreads and ground-spread covers in the Buddhist heartland of ancient India.
A lost verse text possibly included in the Kṣudrakavastu.
One of several types of leather or hide used in ground-spreads and ground-spread covers in the frontier country of Aśmaka.
A cobbler or maker of shoes.

Monks are not allowed to wear “sparkling” footwear. One of several types of footwear prohibited in The Chapter on Leather, along with creaking sandals, tinkling sandals, ram’s horn sandals, sandals like a bodhi leaf, multicolored sandals, and sandals that cost five coins. One of several types of footwear described in The Chapter on Leather is said to “sparkle,” though the exact meaning of this in unclear and no Sanskrit is extant for this term.
A brahmin town to the west, beyond which, along with the adjacent town of Upasthūṇa, the Buddha designated “border regions.”
A fastener used for shoes.
Son and successor of King Kṛkin, who neglects a stūpa built by his father for the relics of the Buddha Kāśyapa.

term.hi: don gyi tshoms kyi mdo sde dag

note.p: A lost verse text possibly included in the Kṣudrakavastu.
One of twelve literary genres found in the Buddhist canon. Inspired utterances generally begin with a brief narrative explaining the circumstances that inspired the verses spoken by the Buddha.

gloss.term: The Verses of the Elder Nuns

term.hi: gnas brtan ma’i tshigs su bcad pa

note.p: A lost verse text possibly included in the Kṣudrakavastu.

term.hi: gnas brtan gyi tshigs su bcad pa

note.p: A lost verse text possibly included in the Kṣudrakavastu.

term.hi: thub pa’i tshigs su bcad pa

note.p: A lost verse text possibly included in the Kṣudrakavastu.

term.hi: ri gnas kyi tshigs su bcad pa

note.p: A verse text included in The Chapter on Medicines -.
The desire realm, form realm, and formless realm.
One of the ascetic practices known as dhūtaguṇas.

Perhaps onomatopoeic. Monks are not allowed to wear footwear made with tinkling sandals. One of several types of footwear prohibited in The Chapter on Leather, along with creaking sandals, sparkling sandals, ram’s horn sandals, sandals like a bodhi leaf, multicolored sandals, and sandals that cost five coins.
The rules that Buddhist monastics have pledged to uphold.

The Buddha’s teachings have traditionally been grouped by subject matter into three classes or “baskets”: the basket of Sūtra or “discourses”; the basket of Vinaya or “monastic discipline”; and the basket of Abhidharma, which examines the philosophical ideas and terms used in the discourses.

Ficus glomerata. A fig tree in which the flower is concealed within the fruit. As the flower of the udumbara tree remains unseen, it has come to symbolize extreme rarity in classical Indian literature.
A brahmin town to the west, beyond which, along with the adjacent town of Sthūṇa, the Buddha designated “border regions.”
A name for Viṣṇu or Kṛṣṇa
A mountain to the north, beyond which the Buddha designated “border regions.”

The ancient capital of the Licchavi republic. The Buddha visited this city several times during his lifetime. It is perhaps most famous as the location where, on different occasions, he cured a plague, admitted the first nuns into the Buddhist order, was offered a bowl of honey by monkeys, and announced his parinirvāṇa three months prior to his departure..
King Prasenajit’s keeper of cattle.

A “vinaya holder” is term that denotes expertise in the canonical Vinaya. Such a vinaya holder is expected to know not only the Prātimokṣa Sūtra but also the Vinayavibhaṅga, in which the monks' rules are explained, and the monastic rites described in the Vinayavastu.

term.hi: bya rgod kyi phung po ri

note.p: A village in the kingdom of Kāśī, whose capital city was Vārāṇasī.
An area set aside within a monastery where monks may practice walking meditation.
One of several types of footwear allowed for Buddhist monks in The Chapter on Leather.
One of several types of footwear allowed for Buddhist monks in The Chapter on Leather.
One of several types of footwear allowed for Buddhist monks in The Chapter on Leather.
One of several types of footwear allowed for Buddhist monks in The Chapter on Leather.

Nowadays, yoga is generally associated with haṭhayoga or one of its derivatives, which emphasize bodily postures and breath control. But the aṣṭāṅgayoga or “eight-limbed yoga” taught by Patañjali in the Yogasūtra includes much more, such as vows, social duties, and obligations. Here, in the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya, yoga refers to the seated practice of meditation.
Late June/early July.
A town and also a river to the south, beyond which the Buddha designated “border regions.”
A famous charnel ground near Bodh Gaya.
Name of a constellation.

term.hi: gro bzhin skyes rna ba bye ba ri

note.p: Son of the wealthy householder Balasena, whose name means “born under Śravaṇa with an ear worth ten million.” His strange encounters on a lengthy sea voyage provide lessons in karmic cause and effect, and he eventually becomes an arhat disciple of the Buddha.

term.hi: gro bzhin skyes bye ba nyi shu pa

note.p: The privileged son of a wealthy merchant who goes forth as a monk under the Buddha. The story of his extreme determination in walking meditation provides the background for the Buddha allowing his monks single-lining sandals.
A tree native to the Indian subcontinent, commonly said to be the type of tree under which the Buddha was born and passed away. Lat. Shorea robusta
